name: Taiwan TSMC Dependency
description: The "Taiwan TSMC Dependency" concept refers to the perceived over-reliance of the global economy, particularly Western nations, on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) for advanced semiconductor manufacturing. Proponents argue this creates a significant geopolitical vulnerability. The theory posits that China's potential military action against Taiwan could cripple global supply chains, causing economic collapse and granting China undue influence. Evidence cited includes TSMC's dominance in advanced chip production (over 50% market share), the essential role of these chips in numerous industries (defense, automotive, consumer electronics), and China's stated ambition to reunify with Taiwan, potentially by force. Some proponents frame this as a deliberate strategy by China to gain economic leverage. Mainstream perspectives acknowledge the concentration of semiconductor manufacturing in Taiwan as a risk, but downplay the likelihood of a catastrophic scenario. Alternative solutions, such as diversifying chip production to other countries (e.g., the US, Europe) and improving supply chain resilience, are actively being pursued. Critics argue that the "dependency" is overstated, pointing to ongoing efforts to develop domestic chip manufacturing capabilities in other nations, and question the assumption that China would necessarily destroy TSMC facilities in the event of military conflict, as doing so would harm their own economy. Furthermore, some argue that a more nuanced understanding of the global semiconductor supply chain reveals complex interdependencies that are not fully captured by the simple 'TSMC dependency' narrative. The concept gains traction within geopolitical analysis and national security circles.
subtitle: Global Semiconductor Supply Chain Vulnerability

claims.evidence: TSMC controls over 50% of the advanced semiconductor market.
claims.criticism: Efforts are underway to diversify chip production outside of Taiwan.

claims.claim: China could exploit TSMC's location for economic leverage.
claims.evidence: China views Taiwan as a renegade province and hasn't ruled out military force.
claims.criticism: Military action is a high-risk strategy for China with potentially devastating consequences for its own economy.

claims.claim: Western economies are overly reliant on Taiwanese semiconductors.
claims.evidence: Critical industries like defense and automotive depend on TSMC-manufactured chips.
claims.criticism: Supply chain diversification and domestic chip manufacturing initiatives are reducing reliance.

keyFigures.name: Chris Miller
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Chip War' - explores the geopolitical implications of semiconductor manufacturing

keyFigures.name: Willy Shih
keyFigures.role: Harvard Business School Professor - critic of the over-reliance narrative

mythologyConnections: The concept of a single point of failure echoes themes in mythology where a hero or kingdom is vulnerable due to a specific weakness.
The idea of a hidden dependency mirrors mythological narratives where seemingly insignificant items or characters hold immense power.

timeline.event: TSMC becomes the dominant player in advanced semiconductor manufacturing.
Increased geopolitical focus on TSMC's role and potential vulnerabilities.

sources.author: Chris Miller
sources.work: Chip War: The Fight for the World's Most Critical Technology
sources.title: Chris Miller

sources.title: Dalibor Rohac
